title: Cystic Fibrosis Pulmonary Exacerbation in a 9-Year-Old Girl (Homozygous F508del, Diagnosed by Newborn Screen) with 1 Week of Increased Productive Cough + Thick Green Sputum + 1.5 kg Weight Loss + Decreased Exercise Tolerance + FEV1 Drop from Baseline 95% to 72% + New Pseudomonas aeruginosa on Sputum Culture: Inpatient IV Antipseudomonal Antibiotics, Intensified Airway Clearance, Pancreatic Enzyme + Fat-Soluble Vitamin + High-Calorie Nutritional Optimization, CFTR Modulator Continuation, and CF-Specific Infection Control
subtitle: CYSTIC FIBROSIS (CF) = autosomal-recessive multisystem disease caused by mutations in the CFTR gene (CF transmembrane conductance regulator — a cAMP-regulated chloride/bicarbonate channel) on chromosome 7; F508del is the most common mutation (~70% of alleles; this patient HOMOZYGOUS F508del); defective CFTR → impaired chloride/bicarbonate transport + sodium/water reabsorption → THICK, VISCOUS, DEHYDRATED SECRETIONS that obstruct exocrine ducts: AIRWAYS (mucus plugging → chronic infection + inflammation + bronchiectasis + progressive obstructive lung disease — the leading cause of morbidity/mortality), PANCREAS (duct obstruction → exocrine pancreatic insufficiency in ~85% → malabsorption of fat + fat-soluble vitamins ADEK + steatorrhea + poor growth; later endocrine → CF-related diabetes), INTESTINE (meconium ileus in neonates + distal intestinal obstruction syndrome DIOS later), HEPATOBILIARY (focal biliary cirrhosis), SWEAT GLANDS (elevated sweat chloride — basis of diagnosis + risk of hyponatremic/hypochloremic dehydration with heat/exercise → SALT supplementation), and REPRODUCTIVE (congenital bilateral absence of vas deferens → male infertility). DIAGNOSIS: NEWBORN SCREEN (elevated immunoreactive trypsinogen IRT → DNA panel) → confirmatory SWEAT CHLORIDE TEST (≥60 mmol/L diagnostic; 30-59 intermediate; pilocarpine iontophoresis) + genetic confirmation. PULMONARY EXACERBATION = acute worsening of respiratory status — INCREASED cough + increased/thicker/darker sputum + DECREASED FEV1 (≥10% from baseline) + increased work of breathing + new crackles/wheeze + decreased appetite/weight loss + decreased exercise tolerance + fatigue + sometimes low-grade fever + hemoptysis; managed with INTENSIFIED airway clearance + antibiotics targeted to the patient's airway organisms (chronic colonizers evolve over time: early Staph aureus + H. influenzae → later PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA — a hallmark CF pathogen that mucoidizes and predicts decline → BURKHOLDERIA CEPACIA complex — associated with rapid decline + 'cepacia syndrome' + transplant contraindication at some centers + non-tuberculous mycobacteria). MANAGEMENT PILLARS (Basic Care & Comfort heavy): (1) AIRWAY CLEARANCE THERAPY (ACT) — daily (intensified during exacerbation) — high-frequency chest wall oscillation (THE VEST), chest physiotherapy (CPT — percussion + postural drainage), positive expiratory pressure (PEP) devices, oscillatory PEP (Flutter/Acapella), active cycle of breathing, autogenic drainage, exercise; (2) INHALED THERAPY SEQUENCE matters — correct order = (a) BRONCHODILATOR (albuterol — opens airways) → (b) MUCOLYTIC/HYDRATOR: DORNASE ALFA (Pulmozyme — recombinant human DNase cleaves extracellular DNA from neutrophils thinning mucus) and/or HYPERTONIC SALINE 7% (osmotically draws water into airway surface; give bronchodilator first to prevent bronchospasm) → (c) AIRWAY CLEARANCE (vest/CPT to mobilize loosened secretions) → (d) INHALED ANTIBIOTIC LAST (tobramycin inhaled/TOBI or aztreonam — deposited in cleared airways for max effect); (3) ANTIBIOTICS for exacerbation — culture-directed; for Pseudomonas, IV DOUBLE-COVERAGE (two antipseudomonals from different classes to broaden coverage + limit resistance — e.g., an antipseudomonal beta-lactam [piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftazidime, cefepime, or meropenem] PLUS an aminoglycoside [tobramycin] or fluoroquinolone) typically 14 days; CF patients need HIGHER doses + may clear drugs faster (altered pharmacokinetics) → therapeutic drug monitoring (aminoglycoside peaks/troughs) + renal/oto/vestibular toxicity surveillance; (4) NUTRITION (central to survival — better nutritional status correlates with better lung function/survival) — PANCREATIC ENZYME REPLACEMENT THERAPY (PERT — lipase/protease/amylase): give with EVERY meal and fat-containing snack, dose by lipase units/kg/meal (do NOT exceed ~2,500 units lipase/kg/meal → fibrosing colonopathy risk), do NOT crush or chew the enteric-coated microspheres (acid-resistant coating must reach the duodenum; may open capsule + sprinkle on acidic soft food like applesauce for young children + give immediately, do not mix in formula/hold), give before/with food not after; HIGH-CALORIE HIGH-FAT diet (110-200% of RDA for healthy children); FAT-SOLUBLE VITAMIN supplementation (A, D, E, K — special CF formulations) since fat malabsorption impairs absorption; SALT supplementation (sodium chloride — losses in sweat, especially heat/exercise/fever); monitor growth (weight/height/BMI percentiles); consider supplemental enteral feeding (G-tube) if poor growth; (5) CFTR MODULATORS — disease-modifying small molecules that correct/potentiate defective CFTR protein: ELEXACAFTOR/TEZACAFTOR/IVACAFTOR (Trikafta — for patients ≥2 yr with ≥1 F508del; dramatically improves FEV1 + reduces exacerbations + improves weight; take with FAT-containing food for absorption; monitor LFTs; drug interactions via CYP3A); continue during exacerbation; (6) INFECTION CONTROL — CF-to-CF transmission of Pseudomonas/Burkholderia is a major concern → patients with CF should NOT be in close contact with each other (no shared rooms/waiting areas/CF events); CONTACT PRECAUTIONS (gown + gloves) + single room + ≥6 ft separation + meticulous hand hygiene + equipment cleaning; (7) ROUTINE PREVENTIVE care — annual influenza + routine immunizations + RSV prophylaxis in infancy + airway culture surveillance + monitoring for CF-related diabetes (annual OGTT from age 10) + bone health + mental-health screening. COMPLICATIONS = bronchiectasis, hemoptysis (can be massive), pneumothorax, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA), respiratory failure (transplant), CF-related diabetes, DIOS, liver disease, osteoporosis. PROGNOSIS markedly improved with modulators + multidisciplinary CF-center care + early aggressive treatment; median survival now into 40s-50s and rising

patient.name: L. Romero
patient.code: Full code; parents are legal decision-makers + provide informed consent; 9-year-old provides developmentally appropriate ASSENT; goals = treat exacerbation + restore baseline lung function + optimize nutrition/growth + maintain school + support child + family
patient.admitDate: Presented to pediatric pulmonology/CF clinic today after 1 WEEK of progressively INCREASED productive COUGH + THICKER GREEN sputum (was usually scant/clear at baseline) + decreased appetite + 1.5 kg WEIGHT LOSS over 2 weeks + decreased exercise tolerance (tires during recess + stopped soccer practice) + intermittent LOW-GRADE FEVERS + increased fatigue + missing school; home spirometry/clinic FEV1 = 72% predicted (DOWN from personal baseline 95% — a >10% drop confirming PULMONARY EXACERBATION); exam = increased work of breathing + new coarse crackles bilateral upper lobes + scattered wheeze; SpO2 93% on room air; sputum sent → culture growing MUCOID PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA (new from prior cultures showing only Staph aureus); admitted to pediatric unit for IV antipseudomonal antibiotics + intensified airway clearance + nutritional optimization + CF-center multidisciplinary care
patient.room: Pediatric unit single room with CONTACT precautions (CF-specific infection control — no contact with other CF patients; gown + gloves + dedicated equipment); child-life + family accommodation; CF multidisciplinary team (pulmonology + CF nurse + respiratory therapy + registered dietitian + pharmacist + social worker + child-life + physical therapy + school liaison)

vitals.hr: 118 (age-appropriate upper range; mild tachycardia with increased WOB/low-grade fever)
vitals.temp: 37.9°C; alert + interactive but tired-appearing + thin; weight 24 kg (down from 25.5 kg; BMI dropped to ~25th percentile from ~40th); increased work of breathing — mild intercostal retractions + nasal flaring with activity; chest = coarse crackles bilateral upper lobes + scattered expiratory wheeze; productive cough with thick green sputum; clubbing of fingertips (chronic hypoxemia); FEV1 72% predicted (baseline 95%)

37.6°C; in single room on CONTACT precautions; sputum culture confirmed MUCOID PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA (sensitivities pending) + scant Staph aureus; CXR = increased peribronchial cuffing + bilateral upper-lobe bronchiectasis + mucus plugging + no pneumothorax/effusion; baseline labs — CBC (mild leukocytosis WBC 14.2 + left shift), CMP, LFTs, fat-soluble vitamin levels (vitamin D low at 22 ng/mL), HbA1c + random glucose (screening for CF-related diabetes — normal), renal function (baseline Cr 0.4 — for aminoglycoside dosing); IV access placed; weight/height/BMI plotted

37.4°C; IV DOUBLE-COVERAGE antipseudomonal antibiotics initiated — CEFTAZIDIME (antipseudomonal cephalosporin) + IV TOBRAMYCIN (aminoglycoside, extended-interval/once-daily dosing with level monitoring); INTENSIFIED airway clearance scheduled QID (vest + correct inhaled-med sequence: albuterol → hypertonic saline 7% + dornase alfa → vest → inhaled tobramycin); home CFTR MODULATOR (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor — Trikafta) CONTINUED with fat-containing food; PANCREATIC ENZYMES (PERT) given with every meal/snack; fat-soluble vitamins + high-calorie diet + salt + dietitian consult; child-life + family support; pain/comfort + energy conservation + clustering of care

37.0°C (afebrile); tolerating QID airway clearance + inhaled therapy in correct sequence; cough productive but sputum thinning + lighter color; appetite improving with enzyme-supported high-calorie meals; tobramycin trough appropriate (renal function stable) + peak therapeutic; sensitivities confirmed Pseudomonas susceptible to both agents; eating + engaging with child-life + schoolwork via hospital teacher; weight stable 24 kg; tolerating Trikafta with meals

36.9°C; clinically much improved — cough decreased + sputum scant/clear + exercise tolerance returning (walking halls + playroom without desaturation) + appetite robust + weight up to 24.6 kg; repeat FEV1 88% predicted (improving toward baseline 95%); tobramycin levels therapeutic + renal function normal + no oto/vestibular symptoms; continuing 14-day IV course (plan for home IV completion via CF home-care vs remainder inpatient per family/teaching); fat-soluble vitamin D repletion started; multidisciplinary discharge planning

36.8°C; completed 14-day IV antipseudomonal course; FEV1 back to 94% (baseline restored); weight 25.2 kg (regained); thriving + back to baseline activity; comprehensive education + teach-back completed (airway clearance + correct inhaled-med sequence + enzymes + vitamins + high-calorie diet + salt + Trikafta with fat + adherence + exacerbation warning signs + infection control + follow-up); discharge home with CF-center follow-up + home airway clearance + return to school + soccer as tolerated; family + child confident

labs.v: 9 y/o girl L. Romero with KNOWN CYSTIC FIBROSIS (diagnosed in newborn period — positive newborn screen [elevated immunoreactive trypsinogen IRT] → confirmatory SWEAT CHLORIDE 102 mmol/L [diagnostic ≥60] + genetic testing HOMOZYGOUS F508del) presenting after 1 WEEK of progressively INCREASED productive COUGH + THICKER GREEN sputum (baseline scant/clear) + decreased appetite + 1.5 kg WEIGHT LOSS over 2 weeks + decreased EXERCISE TOLERANCE (tires at recess + stopped soccer) + intermittent LOW-GRADE FEVERS + increased fatigue + missed school days. BASELINE CF STATUS: pancreatic-insufficient (on enzyme replacement since infancy) + good baseline growth (~40th percentile BMI) + baseline FEV1 ~95% predicted + chronic Staph aureus airway colonization + on CFTR modulator Trikafta (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) started age 6 with excellent response (improved FEV1 + weight + fewer exacerbations) + daily home airway clearance (vest + inhaled hypertonic saline + dornase alfa) + pancreatic enzymes with meals + fat-soluble vitamins (ADEK) + followed at accredited CF center quarterly. PMH: meconium ileus at birth (required surgery) — first clue to CF; multiple prior pulmonary exacerbations (last 8 months ago); never grown Pseudomonas before (cultures previously Staph aureus only) — NEW Pseudomonas is significant. IMMUNIZATIONS up to date + annual influenza. SOCIAL: lives with both parents + younger brother (4, screened negative — not a CF carrier symptomatic) + supportive extended family; mother is primary caregiver + very knowledgeable about CF care + adherent; father works full-time; Spanish + English bilingual home (parents English-proficient; interpreter available); family active in CF community + connected to CF Foundation resources; good insurance + CF-center social work support; child is bright + in 4th grade + loves soccer + art + has good understanding of her CF for age. CLINIC PRESENTATION today: home/clinic FEV1 = 72% predicted (DOWN from personal baseline 95% — a >10% drop = PULMONARY EXACERBATION by definition); exam = increased work of breathing (mild intercostal retractions + nasal flaring with activity) + new coarse crackles bilateral upper lobes + scattered expiratory wheeze; productive cough with thick green sputum; digital clubbing (chronic); SpO2 93% RA; T 37.9°C; weight 24 kg (down from 25.5 kg). PULMONARY EXACERBATION CRITERIA met: increased cough + increased/thicker/darker sputum + ≥10% FEV1 decline + weight loss + decreased exercise tolerance + increased WOB + new auscultatory findings + low-grade fever. ADMITTED for IV antipseudomonal antibiotics + intensified airway clearance + nutritional optimization + CF multidisciplinary care.
labs.r: PULMONARY EXACERBATION of CF (increased cough/sputum + ≥10% FEV1 drop + weight loss + decreased exercise tolerance + new findings) with NEW mucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa — requires aggressive inpatient treatment. RN role: recognize exacerbation criteria + history + escalation + family-centered + developmentally appropriate care.

labs.v: CYSTIC FIBROSIS PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: autosomal-recessive mutation in CFTR gene (chromosome 7) encoding the CFTR protein — a cAMP-regulated CHLORIDE and BICARBONATE channel at epithelial apical membranes. F508del (most common; this patient HOMOZYGOUS) = a Class II processing mutation → misfolded CFTR degraded before reaching the membrane. Defective CFTR → impaired Cl-/HCO3- secretion + excessive Na+/water absorption → dehydrated, thick, viscous secretions that obstruct exocrine ducts across organ systems: (1) RESPIRATORY (dominant cause of morbidity/mortality) — impaired mucociliary clearance → mucus plugging → chronic bacterial infection + neutrophilic inflammation → bronchiectasis → progressive obstructive lung disease → respiratory failure; characteristic airway organism progression (Staph aureus + H. influenzae early → PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA, which mucoidizes + forms biofilm + predicts faster decline → Burkholderia cepacia complex, worst prognosis); (2) PANCREATIC — ductal obstruction → EXOCRINE PANCREATIC INSUFFICIENCY (~85%) → maldigestion/malabsorption of fat + protein + FAT-SOLUBLE VITAMINS (A, D, E, K) → steatorrhea + failure to thrive/poor growth; later ENDOCRINE pancreatic damage → CF-RELATED DIABETES (screen annually with OGTT from age 10); (3) GI — MECONIUM ILEUS in ~15-20% neonates (this patient had it — pathognomonic clue) + DISTAL INTESTINAL OBSTRUCTION SYNDROME (DIOS) later + GERD + rectal prolapse; (4) HEPATOBILIARY — inspissated bile → focal biliary cirrhosis + cholestasis; (5) SWEAT GLANDS — impaired chloride reabsorption → ELEVATED SWEAT CHLORIDE (diagnostic basis) + excessive salt loss in sweat → risk of HYPONATREMIC/HYPOCHLOREMIC DEHYDRATION + metabolic alkalosis with heat/exercise/fever (→ salt supplementation); (6) REPRODUCTIVE — congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (male infertility); reduced fertility in females; (7) BONE — CF-related bone disease/osteoporosis. DIAGNOSIS: (a) NEWBORN SCREEN — elevated IRT → CFTR DNA panel (universal in US); (b) confirmatory SWEAT CHLORIDE TEST via pilocarpine iontophoresis — ≥60 mmol/L diagnostic, 30-59 intermediate (repeat/genetics), <30 normal; (c) GENETIC testing identifying 2 disease-causing CFTR variants; (d) clinical features. This patient: positive NBS → sweat chloride 102 → homozygous F508del confirmed.
labs.r: CF = CFTR chloride/bicarbonate channel defect → thick secretions → multisystem disease: lungs (chronic infection/bronchiectasis), pancreas (exocrine insufficiency + malabsorption + later CF-related diabetes), GI (meconium ileus/DIOS), liver, sweat glands (salt loss), reproductive. Diagnosis = newborn screen → sweat chloride ≥60 + genetics. RN role: understand multisystem scope to anticipate care needs.

labs.v: AIRWAY CLEARANCE THERAPY (ACT) is foundational daily CF care (intensified during exacerbation — often QID inpatient) to mobilize and expel thick airway secretions, prevent mucus plugging, and reduce infection/inflammation. MODALITIES (individualized; often combined): (1) HIGH-FREQUENCY CHEST WALL OSCILLATION ('THE VEST') — inflatable vest vibrates the chest wall to loosen secretions (common + well-tolerated + allows independence); (2) CHEST PHYSIOTHERAPY (CPT) — manual percussion + vibration + POSTURAL DRAINAGE positioning using gravity to drain lung segments; (3) POSITIVE EXPIRATORY PRESSURE (PEP) devices + OSCILLATORY PEP (Flutter, Acapella — vibration + back-pressure splints airways open); (4) ACTIVE CYCLE OF BREATHING + AUTOGENIC DRAINAGE (breathing techniques); (5) EXERCISE (adjunctive — promotes clearance + fitness). TIMING/COORDINATION with inhaled medications is critical — CORRECT SEQUENCE: (a) FIRST inhaled BRONCHODILATOR (albuterol) — opens airways + prevents bronchospasm from subsequent therapies + improves delivery; (b) NEXT inhaled MUCOLYTIC/HYDRATOR — DORNASE ALFA (Pulmozyme — recombinant human DNase that cleaves the extracellular DNA released by degraded neutrophils in CF mucus, reducing viscosity) and/or HYPERTONIC SALINE 7% (osmotic agent that hydrates the airway surface liquid; can cause bronchospasm/cough → always after bronchodilator); (c) THEN AIRWAY CLEARANCE (vest/CPT/PEP) to physically mobilize the now-loosened secretions + cough them out; (d) LAST inhaled ANTIBIOTIC (tobramycin inhalation solution/TOBI or aztreonam lysine) — delivered into AIRWAYS THAT HAVE BEEN CLEARED so the drug reaches the airway surface for maximal antimicrobial effect rather than sitting on top of mucus. RATIONALE summary: open (bronchodilator) → thin/hydrate (dornase/hypertonic saline) → clear (vest/CPT) → treat (inhaled antibiotic). Performing them out of order (e.g., antibiotic before clearance, or hypertonic saline before bronchodilator) reduces efficacy and risks bronchospasm. Oral/inhaled therapy + clearance is scheduled to fit the child's routine (before meals to reduce vomiting + before school + bedtime) and supported with developmentally appropriate engagement (child-life, games, rewards) to promote adherence. NURSING: schedule + coordinate ACT with inhaled meds in correct order, assess sputum (amount/color/consistency) + breath sounds before/after, monitor SpO2 + tolerance, ensure equipment cleaning (infection control), and teach the child + family.
labs.r: Airway clearance (vest/CPT/PEP/breathing techniques + exercise) is daily foundational CF care, intensified in exacerbation. CORRECT inhaled-med SEQUENCE: bronchodilator (open) → dornase alfa/hypertonic saline (thin/hydrate) → airway clearance (mobilize) → inhaled antibiotic LAST (treat cleared airways). RN role: coordinate sequence + assess response + teach + ensure equipment hygiene.

labs.n: Pancreatic enzymes + fat-soluble vitamins + high-calorie nutrition + salt
labs.v: NUTRITION is central to CF survival (nutritional status strongly correlates with lung function + survival): (1) PANCREATIC ENZYME REPLACEMENT THERAPY (PERT) — for the ~85% with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency; enteric-coated microsphere capsules containing LIPASE + PROTEASE + AMYLASE; ADMINISTRATION RULES: give with EVERY meal AND every fat-containing snack (enzymes must be present in the gut WHEN food is — so give at the START of/with the meal, not after); dose by LIPASE UNITS/KG/MEAL (typical 500-2,500 units lipase/kg/meal; DO NOT EXCEED ~2,500 units lipase/kg/meal or ~10,000 units lipase/kg/DAY → higher doses linked to FIBROSING COLONOPATHY); DO NOT CRUSH OR CHEW the enteric-coated beads (the acid-resistant coating must survive gastric acid and dissolve in the alkaline duodenum; crushing destroys it + can irritate oral mucosa); for young children unable to swallow capsules, OPEN the capsule and sprinkle the microspheres on a small amount of ACIDIC SOFT FOOD (e.g., applesauce) and give IMMEDIATELY — do NOT mix into formula/milk or alkaline foods + do not let it sit (coating dissolves) + follow with the meal; titrate to control steatorrhea + optimize growth. (2) FAT-SOLUBLE VITAMIN supplementation (A, D, E, K) — fat malabsorption impairs their absorption → routine CF-specific ADEK supplementation + monitor serum levels (this patient vitamin D low at 22 → repletion); vitamin K especially if liver disease/hemoptysis. (3) HIGH-CALORIE, HIGH-FAT DIET — energy needs 110-200% of the RDA for healthy children due to malabsorption + increased work of breathing + chronic infection; encourage calorie-dense foods + fortification + oral supplements; (4) SALT (SODIUM CHLORIDE) supplementation — CF patients lose excessive sodium and chloride in sweat → risk hyponatremic/hypochloremic dehydration especially with heat, exercise, fever, or vomiting → liberal dietary salt + sodium supplements + ensure hydration; (5) MONITOR GROWTH — weight/height/BMI percentiles every visit (a falling BMI percentile, as in this exacerbation, is a red flag); (6) ENTERAL SUPPORT — if growth remains poor despite optimization, consider supplemental nighttime enteral feeds via gastrostomy; (7) note CFTR modulators (Trikafta) have improved nutritional status, sometimes requiring re-evaluation of caloric targets/weight trends. NURSING: ensure PERT given correctly with all meals/snacks, teach the no-crush/sprinkle-on-applesauce rule, coordinate dietitian, plot growth, encourage high-calorie intake + salt + hydration, and support the child's appetite (treat nausea, time airway clearance before meals, make eating positive).
labs.r: Nutrition is survival-linked in CF. PERT with EVERY meal/snack, dosed by lipase units/kg (cap ~2,500/kg/meal — fibrosing colonopathy), enteric beads NOT crushed (sprinkle on acidic food like applesauce + give immediately if needed); fat-soluble vitamins ADEK; high-calorie high-fat diet (110-200% RDA); salt supplementation (sweat losses); monitor growth percentiles. RN role: correct enzyme administration + teaching + dietitian + growth monitoring.

labs.v: ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY for CF pulmonary exacerbation = CULTURE-DIRECTED based on the patient's airway organisms + sensitivities. For PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA (this patient's new organism — significant + warrants aggressive treatment, especially first isolation when eradication may be attempted): IV DOUBLE-COVERAGE = two antipseudomonal agents from DIFFERENT classes (broadens coverage + reduces emergence of resistance + potential synergy) — typically an ANTIPSEUDOMONAL BETA-LACTAM (piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftazidime, cefepime, meropenem, or aztreonam) PLUS an AMINOGLYCOSIDE (tobramycin) or antipseudomonal fluoroquinolone; this patient on CEFTAZIDIME + IV TOBRAMYCIN; usual duration ~14 days. CF-SPECIFIC PHARMACOKINETICS: CF patients have ALTERED drug handling — larger volume of distribution + faster clearance → require HIGHER doses and/or more frequent dosing than non-CF patients to reach therapeutic levels. AMINOGLYCOSIDE (tobramycin) MONITORING — extended-interval (once-daily) dosing common; monitor PEAK (efficacy) + TROUGH (toxicity) levels; nephrotoxicity (monitor renal function/creatinine + urine output + hydration) + OTOTOXICITY + VESTIBULAR toxicity (assess hearing/tinnitus/balance — can be irreversible; baseline + monitoring audiology with repeated courses); avoid concurrent nephrotoxins. INHALED ANTIBIOTICS (tobramycin inhalation/TOBI, aztreonam lysine) used for chronic Pseudomonas suppression + as part of the inhaled sequence (given LAST after clearance). CFTR MODULATORS — disease-modifying therapy targeting the underlying protein defect: ELEXACAFTOR/TEZACAFTOR/IVACAFTOR (Trikafta) — a triple combination (two correctors + one potentiator) for patients ≥2 yr with at least one F508del allele; dramatically improves FEV1, reduces exacerbations, improves weight/BMI, lowers sweat chloride; ADMINISTRATION: take WITH FAT-CONTAINING FOOD (improves absorption), CONTINUE during exacerbation/hospitalization; monitor LFTs (transaminitis) + baseline + periodic; ophthalmologic monitoring (cataracts) in children; DRUG INTERACTIONS via CYP3A4 (avoid strong inducers like rifampin; adjust with strong inhibitors like azoles); counsel adherence. OTHER chronic therapies: azithromycin (anti-inflammatory/immunomodulatory in chronic Pseudomonas), ibuprofen (high-dose anti-inflammatory in select patients), bronchodilators. MONITORING during admission: daily weight + I&O + respiratory assessment + SpO2 + sputum + FEV1 trend + tobramycin levels + renal function + LFTs (modulator) + glucose (CF-related diabetes screen) + hearing if symptoms.
labs.r: Pseudomonas exacerbation → IV DOUBLE-COVERAGE (antipseudomonal beta-lactam + aminoglycoside) ~14 days; CF patients need HIGHER doses (altered PK); tobramycin requires peak/trough monitoring + renal/oto/vestibular surveillance. CFTR modulator (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) = disease-modifying, take with fat, continue inpatient, monitor LFTs. RN role: administration + therapeutic monitoring + toxicity surveillance + adherence teaching.

labs.n: CF infection control + multidisciplinary + developmental + Pertinent NEGATIVES
labs.v: CF-SPECIFIC INFECTION CONTROL: cross-infection between CF patients with resistant/transmissible organisms (PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA, BURKHOLDERIA CEPACIA complex, MRSA, non-tuberculous mycobacteria) is a serious threat that can accelerate decline → CF patients must NOT have close contact with one another (no shared hospital rooms, waiting areas, clinic times, or CF social events — 'no two CF patients within 6 feet'); inpatient: SINGLE ROOM + CONTACT PRECAUTIONS (gown + gloves) + meticulous HAND HYGIENE + dedicated/cleaned equipment (PFT, vest, stethoscope) + mask when out of room + minimize time in common areas; staff cohorting + cleaning of pulmonary function/clearance equipment. MULTIDISCIPLINARY CF-CENTER care (accredited CF Foundation center improves outcomes): pulmonologist + CF nurse coordinator + RESPIRATORY THERAPIST (airway clearance + inhaled therapy + PFTs) + REGISTERED DIETITIAN (PERT + calories + vitamins + growth) + PHARMACIST (dosing + interactions + adherence) + SOCIAL WORKER (insurance + resources + psychosocial + school) + PHYSICAL THERAPIST + CHILD-LIFE specialist + school liaison + psychology/mental-health screening + genetic counseling. DEVELOPMENTAL + FAMILY-CENTERED care (9-year-old, school-age): provide developmentally appropriate explanations + ASSENT (the child agrees in age-appropriate terms while parents give legal consent); support autonomy + mastery (let her run her vest, choose flavors, participate); maintain SCHOOL (hospital teacher) + play (child-life) + peer connection + routine; address anxiety, body image (clubbing, treatments), and the burden of daily care; support adherence (a major challenge in chronic pediatric disease) through education + routine + positive reinforcement; transition planning over years toward self-management. PERTINENT NEGATIVES: no respiratory failure (SpO2 93-98% + no impending fatigue/CO2 retention + no need for NIV/intubation); no massive hemoptysis (sputum green not bloody — CF can cause significant hemoptysis from bronchiectatic vessels); no pneumothorax (CXR clear of PTX — a CF complication); no Burkholderia cepacia (Pseudomonas, not the worst-prognosis organism); no allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis flare; no CF-related diabetes yet (glucose/HbA1c normal — but screen annually from age 10); no DIOS/bowel obstruction; no liver decompensation; no signs of dehydration/salt depletion crisis; renal function normal (safe aminoglycoside dosing); NKDA. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: known diagnosis + established CF-center care + on CFTR modulator (Trikafta — markedly improves trajectory) + adherent knowledgeable family + good baseline lung function/growth + early recognition of exacerbation + prompt aggressive treatment + first Pseudomonas (eradication opportunity) + intact renal function + strong psychosocial support + good insurance + bright engaged child. Expected: return to baseline FEV1 + weight with 14-day IV course + intensified clearance + nutrition + continued modulator + outpatient follow-up.
labs.r: CF infection control = single room + contact precautions + no CF-to-CF contact (Pseudomonas/Burkholderia cross-infection) + equipment hygiene. Multidisciplinary CF-center team + developmentally appropriate, family-centered, assent-based care + school/play + adherence support. Multiple protective factors (modulator + adherent family + early treatment + first Pseudomonas) support return to baseline. RN role: enforce IC + coordinate team + support child/family.

nursesNote: Clinic/admission 1100 — 9 y/o girl L. Romero with KNOWN CYSTIC FIBROSIS (newborn screen positive [elevated IRT] → sweat chloride 102 mmol/L + HOMOZYGOUS F508del; meconium ileus at birth requiring surgery) presenting after 1 WEEK of progressively INCREASED productive COUGH + THICKER GREEN sputum (baseline scant/clear) + decreased appetite + 1.5 kg WEIGHT LOSS over 2 weeks + decreased EXERCISE TOLERANCE (tires at recess + stopped soccer) + intermittent LOW-GRADE FEVERS + fatigue + missed school. BASELINE: pancreatic-insufficient (enzymes since infancy) + ~40th percentile BMI + FEV1 ~95% + chronic Staph aureus colonization + on CFTR modulator Trikafta since age 6 (excellent response) + daily home airway clearance (vest + hypertonic saline + dornase alfa) + ADEK vitamins + quarterly accredited CF-center follow-up; immunizations UTD + annual flu; multiple prior exacerbations (last 8 mo ago); NEVER grew Pseudomonas before. SOCIAL: lives with both parents + 4 y/o brother (screened, healthy) + supportive extended family; mother primary caregiver + very knowledgeable + adherent; bilingual Spanish/English home (parents English-proficient; interpreter available); active in CF community + CF Foundation resources; good insurance + CF social work; bright 4th-grader who loves soccer + art + understands her CF for age. CLINIC EXAM/VITALS: HR 118 + BP 104/64 + RR 30 (baseline ~22) + SpO2 93% RA + T 37.9°C; alert + interactive but tired + thin; weight 24 kg (down from 25.5; BMI ~25th percentile from ~40th); increased WOB (mild intercostal retractions + nasal flaring with activity); chest = coarse crackles bilateral upper lobes + scattered expiratory wheeze; productive cough thick green sputum; digital clubbing; FEV1 72% predicted (DOWN from baseline 95% — >10% drop = PULMONARY EXACERBATION). Sputum sent → MUCOID PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA (new; prior cultures Staph aureus only). ADMITTED to pediatric unit SINGLE ROOM on CONTACT precautions (CF infection control — no contact with other CF patients; gown + gloves + dedicated equipment) for IV antipseudomonal antibiotics + intensified airway clearance + nutritional optimization + CF multidisciplinary care. DAY 1 0700: sputum confirmed MUCOID PSEUDOMONAS (sensitivities pending) + scant Staph aureus; CXR = increased peribronchial cuffing + bilateral upper-lobe bronchiectasis + mucus plugging + NO pneumothorax/effusion; labs — CBC mild leukocytosis (WBC 14.2 + left shift), CMP/LFTs, fat-soluble vitamin levels (vitamin D LOW 22 ng/mL), HbA1c + random glucose (CF-related diabetes screen — normal), baseline Cr 0.4 (for aminoglycoside dosing); IV access placed; growth plotted. DAY 1 1200 (treatment): IV DOUBLE-COVERAGE antipseudomonal antibiotics initiated — CEFTAZIDIME + IV TOBRAMYCIN (extended-interval/once-daily with peak/trough monitoring); INTENSIFIED airway clearance QID with CORRECT inhaled-med sequence — (1) albuterol bronchodilator → (2) hypertonic saline 7% + dornase alfa → (3) vest airway clearance → (4) inhaled tobramycin LAST; home CFTR MODULATOR (Trikafta) CONTINUED with fat-containing food; PANCREATIC ENZYMES (PERT) with every meal + fat-containing snack (start of meal; capsules NOT crushed — sprinkle on applesauce + give immediately if needed; dose by lipase units/kg, cap ~2,500/kg/meal); FAT-SOLUBLE VITAMINS (ADEK) + vitamin D repletion + HIGH-CALORIE HIGH-FAT diet (110-200% RDA) + SALT supplementation + ensure hydration; dietitian consult; child-life + hospital teacher + family support; energy conservation + clustering care + airway clearance timed BEFORE meals to reduce nausea/vomiting. EDUCATION begun: exacerbation warning signs + correct inhaled sequence + enzyme rules + high-calorie diet + salt + Trikafta with fat + adherence + infection control. DAY 3: afebrile 37.0 + tolerating QID clearance + sputum thinning/lightening + appetite improving + tobramycin trough appropriate (renal stable) + peak therapeutic + sensitivities confirm Pseudomonas susceptible to both agents + engaging with child-life + schoolwork + weight stable 24 kg + tolerating Trikafta with meals. DAY 7: much improved — cough decreased + sputum scant/clear + exercise tolerance returning (halls + playroom without desat) + appetite robust + weight up 24.6 kg + repeat FEV1 88% (toward baseline) + tobramycin levels therapeutic + renal normal + no oto/vestibular symptoms; continuing 14-day IV course (home IV completion vs inpatient per family teaching) + multidisciplinary discharge planning. DAY 14: completed 14-day IV course + FEV1 back to 94% (baseline) + weight 25.2 kg (regained) + thriving + back to baseline activity; comprehensive education + teach-back complete (airway clearance + inhaled sequence + enzymes + vitamins + high-calorie diet + salt + Trikafta with fat + adherence + warning signs + infection control + follow-up); DISCHARGE home with CF-center follow-up + home airway clearance + return to school + soccer as tolerated; family + child confident.
providerOrders: ADMIT to pediatric unit — single room, CONTACT precautions (CF infection control)
DIAGNOSIS: cystic fibrosis (homozygous F508del) pulmonary exacerbation with new mucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa; exocrine pancreatic insufficiency; on CFTR modulator
CODE STATUS: full code; parents consent + developmentally appropriate child assent
INFECTION CONTROL — CF-specific:
Single room + CONTACT precautions (gown + gloves) + meticulous hand hygiene
NO contact with other CF patients (no shared rooms/waiting areas/common areas; >6 ft separation)
Dedicated + cleaned equipment (stethoscope, PFT, vest); mask when out of room; minimize common-area time
DIAGNOSTICS:
Sputum culture + sensitivities (DONE: mucoid Pseudomonas aeruginosa + scant Staph aureus; sensitivities pending → both susceptible)
Chest X-ray (DONE: peribronchial cuffing + upper-lobe bronchiectasis + mucus plugging; no PTX/effusion)
Spirometry FEV1 on admission + serial (track recovery toward baseline 95%)
CBC with differential, CMP, LFTs (modulator), fat-soluble vitamin levels (A/D/E/K), HbA1c + random glucose (CF-related diabetes screen)
Baseline serum creatinine + renal function (aminoglycoside dosing); daily during tobramycin
Daily weight; height; BMI percentile
ANTIBIOTICS — IV double-coverage antipseudomonal (~14 days):
CEFTAZIDIME IV (antipseudomonal cephalosporin) — CF-adjusted higher dosing
TOBRAMYCIN IV (aminoglycoside) — extended-interval (once-daily) dosing; CF-adjusted
Monitor peak (efficacy) + trough (toxicity) levels; daily renal function; assess hearing/tinnitus/balance (oto/vestibular); avoid concurrent nephrotoxins; ensure hydration
AIRWAY CLEARANCE + INHALED THERAPY — QID (intensified), in CORRECT SEQUENCE:
1) Albuterol (bronchodilator — open airways first)
2) Hypertonic saline 7% + dornase alfa (thin/hydrate secretions; after bronchodilator to prevent bronchospasm)
3) Airway clearance — high-frequency chest wall oscillation vest (and/or CPT/PEP) to mobilize secretions
4) Inhaled tobramycin LAST (treat cleared airways)
Time airway clearance BEFORE meals to reduce nausea/vomiting; assess sputum + breath sounds + SpO2 before/after; clean equipment
CFTR MODULATOR:
CONTINUE home elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Trikafta) — give WITH fat-containing food
Monitor LFTs; reconcile drug interactions (CYP3A — avoid strong inducers)
NUTRITION (dietitian consult):
PANCREATIC ENZYME REPLACEMENT (PERT) with EVERY meal + fat-containing snack — at start of meal
Dose by lipase units/kg/meal; do NOT exceed ~2,500 units lipase/kg/meal (fibrosing colonopathy)
Do NOT crush/chew enteric-coated beads; if unable to swallow capsule, sprinkle microspheres on small amount of acidic soft food (applesauce) + give immediately + follow with meal
Fat-soluble vitamins A/D/E/K (CF formulation); vitamin D repletion (level 22 → supplement)
High-calorie, high-fat diet (110-200% RDA); calorie-dense foods + oral supplements
SALT (sodium chloride) supplementation; ensure hydration (sweat sodium/chloride losses)
Monitor growth (weight/BMI); consider enteral support if poor growth
SUPPORTIVE + MONITORING:
Vital signs + SpO2 with respiratory assessment; supplemental O2 only if needed to maintain SpO2
I&O; daily weight; assess for dehydration/salt depletion
Energy conservation + cluster care + rest; positioning for comfort + drainage
Oral care; skin care
Antipyretic/analgesic for comfort PRN
MULTIDISCIPLINARY (CF-center team):

Pulmonology + CF nurse coordinator + respiratory therapy + registered dietitian + pharmacist + social worker + physical therapy + child-life + hospital school teacher + psychology/mental-health screening + genetic counseling as needed
DEVELOPMENTAL + FAMILY-CENTERED:
Age-appropriate explanations + child assent + autonomy/mastery (let her run vest, choose flavors, participate)
Maintain school (hospital teacher) + play (child-life) + routine + peer connection
Support adherence + address anxiety/body image; bilingual support/interpreter available
Family education + teach-back; involve mother (primary caregiver) + father
PATIENT + FAMILY EDUCATION + TEACH-BACK:
Exacerbation warning signs (increased cough/sputum change/decreased activity/appetite/weight/fever) → when to call CF center
Correct inhaled-medication sequence (bronchodilator → hypertonic saline/dornase → clearance → inhaled antibiotic)
Daily airway clearance technique + equipment cleaning
PERT rules (every meal/snack, no crush, sprinkle on applesauce, dose limits) + fat-soluble vitamins + high-calorie diet + salt + hydration
Trikafta with fat-containing food + adherence + LFT monitoring
Infection control (avoid other CF patients; hand hygiene; equipment hygiene)
Immunizations (annual influenza; routine) + CF-related diabetes screening from age 10
DISCHARGE PLANNING (when clinically recovered ~day 14):
Complete 14-day IV course (home IV via CF home-care vs remainder inpatient per family teaching/competence)
CF-center follow-up ~1-2 weeks + repeat spirometry + airway culture surveillance
Resume home airway clearance + inhaled regimen + enzymes + vitamins + Trikafta
Return to school + soccer/activity as tolerated; school liaison communication
Social work follow-up (resources/insurance); when to seek care (massive hemoptysis, severe dyspnea, signs of dehydration)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this CF pulmonary exacerbation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: PULMONARY EXACERBATION criteria — increased productive cough + thicker/darker (green) sputum + FEV1 drop from baseline 95% to 72% (>10% decline) + 1.5 kg weight loss + decreased exercise tolerance + increased work of breathing + new bilateral crackles + low-grade fever
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — defines a CF pulmonary exacerbation requiring intensified treatment. RN role: recognition + escalation.

opts.text: NEW mucoid PSEUDOMONAS AERUGINOSA on sputum culture (previously only Staph aureus) — a hallmark CF pathogen that predicts decline; first isolation warrants aggressive culture-directed antibiotics (IV double-coverage) + possible eradication
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — new Pseudomonas is significant + drives antibiotic selection. RN role: culture interpretation + escalation.

opts.text: AIRWAY CLEARANCE must be intensified + coordinated with inhaled meds in the CORRECT sequence — bronchodilator → hypertonic saline/dornase alfa → airway clearance (vest/CPT) → inhaled antibiotic LAST
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — airway clearance + correct inhaled sequence are foundational CF therapy. RN role: coordinate + assess + teach.

opts.text: NUTRITION + falling BMI percentile — pancreatic enzyme replacement with every meal/snack + fat-soluble vitamins (ADEK; vitamin D low) + high-calorie high-fat diet + salt supplementation; nutritional status correlates with lung function/survival
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — nutrition is survival-linked in CF; the weight loss/falling BMI is a red flag. RN role: enzyme administration + dietitian + growth monitoring.

opts.text: TOBRAMYCIN (aminoglycoside) requires therapeutic drug monitoring (peak/trough) + renal function + oto/vestibular surveillance; CF patients need higher/adjusted doses due to altered pharmacokinetics
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — aminoglycoside efficacy/toxicity monitoring + CF-specific dosing. RN role: level monitoring + toxicity surveillance + safe administration.

opts.text: CF-SPECIFIC INFECTION CONTROL — single room + contact precautions + NO contact with other CF patients (cross-infection with Pseudomonas/Burkholderia) + equipment hygiene + hand hygiene
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — CF cross-infection prevention is critical. RN role: enforce isolation + equipment hygiene + education.

opts.text: CFTR modulator (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor / Trikafta) should be CONTINUED during admission + given with fat-containing food; monitor LFTs + drug interactions
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — disease-modifying therapy continued inpatient + administration details. RN role: administration with food + monitoring + adherence.

opts.text: DEVELOPMENTALLY APPROPRIATE + family-centered care — 9-year-old needs age-appropriate explanations + assent + autonomy/mastery + school + play + adherence support; knowledgeable adherent family + bilingual support
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — school-age developmental + family-centered care supports coping + adherence. RN role: developmental support + family engagement + child-life/school.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. No medication contraindications. Important for safe antibiotic administration though not the priority cue.

opts.text: Crush the pancreatic enzyme capsules and mix the beads into a bottle of warm milk so they dissolve fully before the meal for better absorption
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Enteric-coated enzyme beads must NOT be crushed/chewed (destroys the acid-resistant coating + irritates oral mucosa) and should NOT be mixed into milk/warm or alkaline liquids. If the child cannot swallow the capsule, open it and sprinkle microspheres on a small amount of ACIDIC soft food (applesauce) + give immediately.

opts.text: Give the inhaled antibiotic (tobramycin) FIRST, before the bronchodilator and airway clearance, so the medication has the longest time in the lungs
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG sequence. The inhaled antibiotic goes LAST — after bronchodilator (open) + hypertonic saline/dornase (thin) + airway clearance (mobilize/expel) — so it deposits in CLEARED airways. Giving it first leaves it sitting on top of mucus, reducing efficacy.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What CF pathophysiology + management principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: CF = CFTR chloride/bicarbonate channel defect (homozygous F508del) → thick dehydrated secretions → multisystem disease: airways (mucus plugging + chronic infection + bronchiectasis), pancreas (exocrine insufficiency → malabsorption + poor growth), GI (meconium ileus/DIOS), liver, sweat glands (salt loss), reproductive
opts.rat: CORRECT — CF pathophysiology + multisystem scope. Foundation for anticipating care needs.

opts.text: Pulmonary exacerbation = acute worsening (increased cough/sputum + ≥10% FEV1 decline + weight loss + decreased exercise tolerance + new findings ± fever) → intensified airway clearance + culture-directed antibiotics; new Pseudomonas warrants aggressive IV therapy
opts.rat: CORRECT — exacerbation definition + management framework. Foundation for treatment.

opts.text: Correct inhaled-medication SEQUENCE: bronchodilator (open) → dornase alfa/hypertonic saline (thin/hydrate; saline after bronchodilator to prevent bronchospasm) → airway clearance (mobilize) → inhaled antibiotic LAST (treat cleared airways)
opts.rat: CORRECT — inhaled sequence rationale. Foundation for effective therapy delivery.

opts.text: Pancreatic enzymes (PERT) with every meal/snack, dosed by lipase units/kg (cap ~2,500/kg/meal — fibrosing colonopathy), enteric beads NOT crushed; plus fat-soluble vitamins ADEK + high-calorie high-fat diet + salt — because exocrine pancreatic insufficiency causes fat/vitamin malabsorption + sweat causes salt loss
opts.rat: CORRECT — nutrition/PERT principles tied to pathophysiology. Foundation for growth + survival.

opts.text: Pseudomonas exacerbation → IV double-coverage (antipseudomonal beta-lactam + aminoglycoside) for broadened coverage + reduced resistance; CF patients need higher/adjusted doses (altered PK); tobramycin needs peak/trough + renal/oto/vestibular monitoring
opts.rat: CORRECT — antipseudomonal double-coverage + CF pharmacokinetics + aminoglycoside monitoring. Foundation for safe effective therapy.

opts.text: CFTR modulators (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) target the underlying protein defect → improve FEV1 + weight + reduce exacerbations; take with fat-containing food + continue inpatient + monitor LFTs
opts.rat: CORRECT — modulator mechanism + administration. Foundation for disease-modifying therapy.

opts.text: CF-to-CF cross-infection (Pseudomonas/Burkholderia cepacia) accelerates decline → single room + contact precautions + no contact between CF patients + equipment hygiene
opts.rat: CORRECT — CF infection-control principle. Foundation for preventing cross-infection.

opts.text: Because the child looks well between exacerbations, daily airway clearance and enzymes can be skipped when she feels fine to reduce treatment burden
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + harmful. CF requires CONSISTENT daily airway clearance + enzymes with all meals regardless of how she feels; skipping accelerates mucus plugging, infection, malabsorption, and decline. Adherence between exacerbations is essential.

opts.text: Antibiotics should be stopped as soon as the fever resolves and the cough improves (a few days) to limit antibiotic exposure
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. CF exacerbation IV courses are typically ~14 days; stopping early risks incomplete treatment + relapse + resistance, especially with Pseudomonas. Complete the full prescribed course.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns at this CF exacerbation
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: AIRWAY CLEARANCE + RESPIRATORY status r/t mucus plugging + Pseudomonas exacerbation + decreased FEV1 + increased WOB + SpO2 93% — intensified airway clearance with correct inhaled sequence + assess breath sounds/sputum/SpO2 + supplemental O2 if needed
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Airway/breathing is the immediate physiologic priority; clearing secretions + treating infection restores oxygenation/lung function. RN role: airway clearance + respiratory monitoring.

opts.text: EFFECTIVE ANTIBIOTIC THERAPY + safe monitoring r/t new Pseudomonas — IV double-coverage (ceftazidime + tobramycin) + aminoglycoside peak/trough + renal/oto/vestibular surveillance + complete 14-day course
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Treating the infection drives recovery; aminoglycoside requires careful monitoring. RN role: administration + therapeutic monitoring + toxicity surveillance.

opts.text: NUTRITION + growth r/t weight loss + falling BMI + pancreatic insufficiency — enzymes with every meal/snack + fat-soluble vitamins + high-calorie diet + salt + hydration; nutritional status correlates with lung function/survival
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Nutrition is survival-linked + the weight loss is a red flag; supports recovery. RN role: enzyme administration + dietitian + growth monitoring + appetite support.

opts.text: INFECTION CONTROL r/t CF cross-infection risk — single room + contact precautions + no contact with other CF patients + equipment hygiene + hand hygiene + continue CFTR modulator
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Preventing cross-infection protects this patient + other CF patients. RN role: enforce isolation + equipment hygiene + education.

opts.text: DEVELOPMENTAL + FAMILY-CENTERED support + adherence r/t 9-year-old + school + play + chronic-disease burden — age-appropriate explanations + assent + autonomy + child-life + school + family education + bilingual support + adherence reinforcement
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + for long-term success. Coping + adherence support sustains lifelong management. RN role: developmental + family-centered care + education.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this CF pulmonary exacerbation.

opts.text: Intensified airway clearance (QID vest/CPT) coordinated with inhaled meds in correct sequence: albuterol → hypertonic saline 7% + dornase alfa → airway clearance → inhaled tobramycin last; assess sputum/breath sounds/SpO2 before and after
opts.rat: Foundational CF exacerbation therapy with correct sequencing. RN role: coordinate + assess + teach.

opts.text: IV double-coverage antipseudomonal antibiotics (ceftazidime + tobramycin) with CF-adjusted dosing; monitor tobramycin peak/trough + daily renal function + hearing/balance; complete ~14-day course
opts.rat: Standard culture-directed therapy for Pseudomonas exacerbation + aminoglycoside monitoring. RN role: administration + monitoring.

opts.text: Pancreatic enzymes with every meal and fat-containing snack (at start of meal), dosed by lipase units/kg without exceeding ~2,500 units/kg/meal; do not crush beads (sprinkle on applesauce if needed)
opts.rat: Correct PERT administration for pancreatic insufficiency. RN role: administer correctly + teach.

opts.text: Fat-soluble vitamins (A/D/E/K) + vitamin D repletion + high-calorie high-fat diet (110-200% RDA) + salt supplementation + ensure hydration; monitor weight/BMI; dietitian consult
opts.rat: Standard CF nutritional optimization. RN role: support intake + administer vitamins/salt + dietitian + growth monitoring.

opts.text: Continue home CFTR modulator (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) with fat-containing food; monitor LFTs and reconcile drug interactions
opts.rat: Disease-modifying therapy should continue inpatient with correct administration. RN role: administer with food + monitor.

opts.text: Single room + contact precautions + no contact with other CF patients + dedicated/cleaned equipment + hand hygiene
opts.rat: CF-specific infection control to prevent cross-infection. RN role: enforce + educate.

opts.text: Developmentally appropriate explanations + child assent + child-life + hospital school + family education with teach-back + bilingual support + adherence reinforcement
opts.rat: Family-centered, developmentally appropriate care supports coping + adherence. RN role: developmental support + education.

opts.text: Time airway clearance before meals + manage nausea + offer calorie-dense preferred foods to support appetite and reduce vomiting
opts.rat: Practical comfort + nutrition strategy. RN role: schedule care + support intake + comfort.

opts.text: Crush enzyme capsules into warm milk; give inhaled antibiotic before bronchodilator and airway clearance; skip clearance on days she feels well to reduce burden
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG on all counts. Don't crush enteric beads or mix in milk; inhaled antibiotic goes LAST after clearance; daily clearance must continue regardless of how she feels.

opts.text: Place the patient in a shared room with another child who has CF so they can support each other during their hospital stays
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. CF patients must NOT be in contact with one another due to cross-infection risk (Pseudomonas/Burkholderia). Single room + contact precautions required.

opts.text: Stop the IV antibiotics after 3-4 days once the fever resolves and the cough improves to limit antibiotic exposure
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Complete the full ~14-day course; stopping early risks relapse + resistance, especially with Pseudomonas.

opts.text: Restrict dietary fat and salt to reduce GI symptoms and avoid fluid retention
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG for CF. CF requires HIGH-fat, HIGH-calorie intake (with enzymes) + EXTRA salt (sweat losses). Fat/salt restriction worsens malnutrition + risks salt-depletion dehydration.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Action sequence for this CF exacerbation admission
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Place in single room on contact precautions (CF infection control) + complete respiratory assessment (breath sounds, WOB, SpO2, sputum) + obtain sputum culture + establish IV access + notify CF team
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Isolate (protect others) + assess airway/breathing + get culture + access for therapy. RN role: isolation + assessment + setup.

opts.text: Begin intensified airway clearance with correct inhaled sequence (albuterol → hypertonic saline/dornase → vest → inhaled tobramycin) + assess response (sputum, breath sounds, SpO2)
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Mobilizing/expelling secretions improves oxygenation + lung function. RN role: coordinate sequence + assess.

opts.text: Administer IV double-coverage antibiotics (ceftazidime + tobramycin) with CF-adjusted dosing + draw tobramycin levels + monitor renal function/hearing; continue CFTR modulator with food
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Treat the infection + monitor aminoglycoside safety + continue modulator. RN role: administration + monitoring.

opts.text: Optimize nutrition — enzymes with every meal/snack + fat-soluble vitamins + high-calorie high-fat diet + salt + hydration; dietitian consult; time clearance before meals; monitor weight/BMI
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Nutrition supports recovery + is survival-linked. RN role: enzyme administration + dietitian + intake support + growth monitoring.

opts.text: Provide developmentally appropriate + family-centered support — assent + child-life + hospital school + family education with teach-back + adherence reinforcement + discharge planning
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing + foundational. Coping + adherence + education sustain long-term management. RN role: developmental + family-centered care + education + discharge planning.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1: in single room on contact precautions; IV ceftazidime + tobramycin started with level monitoring; QID airway clearance in correct inhaled sequence; CFTR modulator continued with food; enzymes/vitamins/high-calorie diet/salt initiated; dietitian + child-life + school engaged
opts.rat: Correct comprehensive CF exacerbation management initiated across all pillars. Foundation for recovery.

opts.text: Day 3: afebrile; sputum thinning + lightening; appetite improving; tobramycin peak therapeutic + trough appropriate with stable renal function; Pseudomonas confirmed susceptible to both agents; weight stable; engaging with child-life/school
opts.rat: Expected early response to therapy with safe aminoglycoside levels + culture confirmation. Foundation for continued recovery.

opts.text: Day 7: cough decreased + sputum scant/clear; exercise tolerance returning without desaturation; appetite robust + weight up; FEV1 improved to 88% (toward baseline 95%); tobramycin levels therapeutic + no oto/vestibular symptoms
opts.rat: Clear clinical + spirometric improvement with safe drug monitoring. Foundation for completion + discharge.

opts.text: Day 14: completed 14-day IV course; FEV1 back to baseline 94%; weight regained to 25.2 kg; thriving + back to baseline activity; education/teach-back complete; discharged home with CF-center follow-up
opts.rat: Excellent outcome — return to baseline lung function + weight + successful education + safe discharge. Foundation for outpatient management.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — enzymes were crushed and mixed into warm milk for convenience; the child develops worsening steatorrhea + abdominal pain + ongoing weight loss because the destroyed enteric coating left the enzymes inactivated
opts.rat: ⚠️ Improper PERT administration (crushing/mixing in milk destroys the enteric coating) → malabsorption persists. Demonstrates why beads are never crushed (sprinkle on acidic food if needed) + given with every meal.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the inhaled antibiotic was given first (before bronchodilator and airway clearance) and clearance was skipped on 'good' days; two weeks later FEV1 has not recovered and sputum remains thick and copious
opts.rat: ⚠️ Wrong inhaled sequence + inconsistent clearance → poor secretion mobilization + reduced drug deposition → failure to recover. Demonstrates importance of correct sequence + consistent daily clearance.

opts.text: Day 5 alternative — the child reports new tinnitus + a rising serum creatinine while on tobramycin; the team holds the dose, rechecks levels/renal function, ensures hydration, and consults pharmacy/audiology
opts.rat: ⚠️ Aminoglycoside oto-/nephrotoxicity signals. Demonstrates why peak/trough + renal + hearing monitoring matter; appropriate response (hold + reassess) prevents permanent injury.

opts.text: Day 2 alternative — the patient was placed in a shared room with another CF patient; weeks later both grow a new transmissible Burkholderia cepacia, and one declines rapidly
opts.rat: ⚠️ CF-to-CF cross-infection from shared room. Demonstrates why CF patients require single rooms + contact precautions + no contact with each other. Triggers infection-control review.

opts.text: 1-month follow-up — completed treatment, FEV1 at baseline, weight tracking up, back at school + soccer, adherent to home airway clearance + enzymes + vitamins + Trikafta; routine airway surveillance culture obtained
opts.rat: Standard expected outpatient recovery + adherence + surveillance. Foundation for long-term stability.

opts.text: Day 1 vitals + FEV1 (SpO2 93%, RR 30, FEV1 72%, weight 24 kg) compared with the same values on day 1 several hours later before any therapy has taken effect — unchanged
opts.rat: Before interventions take effect, the exacerbation findings remain at presentation baseline — neither improved nor worsened yet. Reassessment after therapy is needed to judge trajectory.
